title: Variables and Data Types Applied Check
description: Test your understanding of references, mutation, Symbols, BigInt, and type conversion versus coercion.
author: DailyCoder

questions.explanation: typeof null returns "object" because of a historical JavaScript bug, even though null is a primitive.

questions.prompt: What is the result of `Symbol("id") === Symbol("id")`?
questions.explanation: Every call to Symbol() creates a new unique symbol, even when the descriptions match.
Appending n to a whole-number literal creates a BigInt. BigInt cannot represent decimals and is not used with new.
JavaScript does not allow mixing BigInt and number values in arithmetic without explicit conversion.

questions.prompt: What is printed after this code? `const a = [1, 2]; const b = a; b.push(3); console.log(a.length);`

options.text: It throws an error because a is const

questions.explanation: Arrays are reference types. a and b point to the same array, and const does not prevent mutation.

questions.prompt: Which creates a shallow copy of an object?
questions.explanation: Object spread creates a shallow copy of an object. Assignment only copies the reference.
When + sees a string operand, JavaScript performs string concatenation.
The subtraction operator only performs math, so JavaScript coerces "10" to the number 10.

questions.prompt: Which comparisons evaluate to true? (Select all that apply.)
questions.explanation: Loose equality performs coercion, so 5 == "5" and null == undefined are true. Strict equality checks type too.

questions.prompt: Which conversion is explicit rather than implicit?
questions.explanation: Number("42") is explicit conversion because the developer directly asks JavaScript to convert the value.
